title: Text lives in a string
instruction:
Think about a name tag at a conference. It has text on it — your name, your company, maybe a title. It is not a number. You cannot do math with it. It is just... text.

In Go, any piece of text is called a string. Your name is a string. A sentence is a string. An email address is a string. Even the number "42" written inside quotes is a string — because it is just the characters 4 and 2, not a real number you can calculate with.

You recognize a string by two things: it is always wrapped in double quotes.

    favoriteFood := "Pizza"

Create a variable called favoriteFood and put your actual favorite food in it as a string. Then print it.
hint: favoriteFood := "Pizza"  — whatever your favorite food is goes inside the double quotes. Then fmt.Println(favoriteFood).

codeChecks.message: Create a string variable: favoriteFood := "Pizza" (use your real favorite food, in double quotes)
Print it: fmt.Println(favoriteFood)
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: Counting things needs an int
instruction:
You have 3 brothers. You are 24 years old. Your team scored 87 points. The year is 2025.

Notice what all of these have in common: they are whole numbers. No decimal point. You can count them on your fingers (well, maybe not 2025, but you get the idea).

In Go, whole numbers are called int. No quotes around them — they are real numbers, not text.

    age := 24

See the difference from a string? No quotes. Just the number.

Create a variable called age with your actual age as a whole number. Then print it.
hint: age := 24  — just the number, nothing else. No quotes, no decimal point. Then fmt.Println(age).

codeChecks.message: Create an int variable: age := 24 (your real age, no quotes, no decimal)
Print it: fmt.Println(age)
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: Some numbers need a decimal point
instruction:
You walk into a coffee shop. Your latte costs $4.75. You are not paying $4 or $5 — you are paying exactly $4.75.

Whole numbers cannot represent that. 4 is not 4.75. You need something that can handle the part after the dot.

In Go, numbers with decimal points are called float64. Prices, temperatures, GPAs, heights — anything that needs precision uses float64.

    price := 4.75

The moment Go sees a dot in your number, it automatically treats it as float64. No extra keyword needed.

Create a variable called price with any price you like — make it realistic, something with a decimal point. Then print it.
hint: price := 4.99  — the decimal point is everything here. No quotes. Then fmt.Println(price).

codeChecks.message: Create a float64 variable: price := 4.99 (must include a decimal point, no quotes)
Print it: fmt.Println(price)
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: Yes or no — that is a bool
instruction:
Is the light on? Yes or no. Are you logged in? Yes or no. Is the task done? Yes or no.

Some questions in life only have two possible answers. In Go, we have a type built exactly for that: bool.

A bool can only ever hold one of two values: true or false. Nothing else. Not "yes". Not 1. Just the words true or false, with no quotes around them.

    isLoggedIn := true
    taskDone := false

Why no quotes? Because true and false are special keywords in Go, not text. If you write "true" in quotes, Go thinks it is a string — just the four letters t, r, u, e. Very different thing.

Create a variable called isWeekend. Set it to true if today is a weekend, false if it is a weekday. Then print it.
hint: isWeekend := true  or  isWeekend := false  — no quotes around true or false. Then fmt.Println(isWeekend).

codeChecks.message: Create a bool variable: isWeekend := true  (or false — no quotes, these are not strings)
Print it: fmt.Println(isWeekend)
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: Your complete profile
instruction:
Think of a user profile on any app — Instagram, LinkedIn, a game. It stores different kinds of information about you: your name (text), your age (whole number), your rating or score (decimal), whether you are verified (yes or no).

That is exactly what you are building now. Four variables, four different types.

Create all four:
- name — a string with your name
- age — an int with your age (whole number, no quotes)
- height — a float64 with your height in meters like 1.75 (must have a decimal)
- isStudent — a bool, true if you are a student, false if not (no quotes)

Then print all four, one per line.

You are doing great. This is what real programs look like.
hint: Four := lines, four Println lines. name gets quotes, age gets a plain number, height gets a decimal, isStudent gets true or false with no quotes.

codeChecks.message: Create name as a string: name := "YourName" (in double quotes)
Create age as an int: age := 24 (no quotes, no decimal)
Create height as a float64: height := 1.75 (must have a decimal point)
Create isStudent as a bool: isStudent := true  (or false, no quotes)
Print name: fmt.Println(name)
Print age: fmt.Println(age)
Print height: fmt.Println(height)
Print isStudent: fmt.Println(isStudent)
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.
